Zij nu, die uitsluitend den room willen karnen, moeten de melk korter laten bekoelen, daar zij anders te veel beroomd, en de te veel beroomde melk, in de aden overgegoten, minder room afscheidt, dan wanneer zij minder beroomd is. De melk 1 à 2 etmalen in de aden gestaan hebbende, neemt men den room met een zeer dun houten bord, dat vooraf in koud water gelegen heeft, van de melk af, en brengt hem in een molkenvat over, dat men tot aan het karnen in den kelder plaatst; is echter de room niet koud genoeg, dan koelt men hem eerst nog af, voor dat hij in het vat gegoten wordt.

In de friesche kelders, waar men de aden op eene melkstelling rond den kelder plaatst, heeft elke aad een gemiddelde inhoud van 20 ad 25 liters, bij een oppervlak ½ □ M. zoodat men per koe ééne aad kan rekenen.

aron.e: Borrowed from Latin aron (also arum), from Ancient Greek ἄρον (áron).

afscheiden.e: From Middle Dutch afscheden, from Old Dutch *afskeithan, from Proto-West Germanic *abaskaiþan, from Proto-Germanic *abaskaiþaną. Equivalent to af + scheiden.

aars.e: From Middle Dutch āers, from Old Dutch *ars, from Proto-West Germanic *ars, from Proto-Germanic *arsaz, from Proto-Indo-European *h₁órsos.

automobilist.e: Borrowed from French automobiliste. Equivalent to automobiel + -ist.

aker.e: From Middle Dutch aker, eker, haker, from Old Dutch *aker, from Proto-West Germanic [Term?], Latin aquarium. The loan from Latin is likely very early, as evidenced by the shifted consonants in Old High German ahhāri, ahari, agari. Doublet of aquarium.

altist.e: Borrowed from Italian altista. Equivalent to alt + -ist.

amer.e: Likely etymologically identical to Eem. See also Amersfoort.

afbijten.e: From Middle Dutch afbiten. Equivalent to af + bijten.

s.def: a particular variety or composition of petroleum

examples: Zonnevliegtuig legt recordafstand af.

s.def: to prepare a deceased person for the funeral

afleggen.e: From Middle Dutch afleggen, aveleggen, from Proto-Germanic *abalagjaną. Equivalent to af + leggen.

s.def: second/third-person singular dependent-clause present indicative of afleggen
a village in Lochem, Gelderland, Netherlands

almen.e: First attested as almen in 1188. Likely derived from Middle Dutch olm (“elm, Ulmus”). Compare Almelo.

astrid.e: Borrowed from modern Scandinavian (Swedish, Norwegian, Danish) Astrid.

antropologie.e: From antropo-, from Ancient Greek ἄνθρωπος (ánthrōpos, “man, mankind, human, humanity”) + -logie.

s.def: abduction, act of abducing (movement away from the median axis of the body)

s.def: to appoint, to commission, to name for a role

afvoeren.e: From Middle Dutch afvoeren. Equivalent to af + voeren.

acroniem.e: Borrowed from English acronym, from Ancient Greek ἄκρος (ákros, “extremity”) and ὄνομα (ónoma, “name”). Equivalent to acro- + -niem.

acceleratie.e: Borrowed from Middle French accélération, from Latin accelerātio. Equivalent to accelereren + -atie.

aarden.e: From Middle Dutch erdijn, from Old Dutch *irthīn, from Proto-West Germanic *irþīn, from Proto-Germanic *irþīnaz, equivalent to aarde + -en. Reformed by analogy with aarde, undoing the effects of umlaut.

s.def: cast, an object made by pouring liqui(fie)d matter in a mould

afweren.e: From Middle Dutch afwēren. Equivalent to af + weren.

aanschouwen.e: From Middle Dutch aenschouwen. Equivalent to aan- + schouwen.

anderen.e:
First attested as anderne in 1217. Etymology unknown. The toponym may be of pre-Germanic origin.
See also Dutch Low Saxon Aandern.

algoritme.e: From Medieval Latin algorithmus, a by-form of algorismus introduced under the influence of Ancient Greek ἀριθμός (arithmós, “number”). A Latinization of Arabic الْخُوَارِزْمِيّ (al-ḵuwārizmiyy, “native of Khowarizm”), surname of the Persian mathematician Muhammad ibn Mūsā al-Khwārizmī.

autobiografie.e: Borrowed from French autobiographie. Equivalent to auto- + biografie.

assen.e: First attested as ascen in 1267-1268. Etymology unknown. Not derived from a personal name or Old Saxon asna (“rent, wage”).

s.def: to demarcate, mark out with boundaries
to sell (products or commodities, typically at a specific sales market)
to close off, to barricade (a street)
to dethrone, to remove from office

afzetten.e: From Middle Dutch afsetten. Equivalent to af + zetten.

examples: Ik vind het al vreselijk om ze alleen maar te zien.

Na zijn heldendaad werd hij geadeld door de vorst.

afbreken.e: From Middle Dutch afbreken. Equivalent to af + breken.

achterwaarts.e: From Middle Dutch achterwaert with later -s. Equivalent to achter + -waarts.

derivative (a word which has been derived from another one)

to take heed, to take an interest in something, to let oneself be bothered by
examples: Trek je maar niets aan van hun geschreeuw.

aantrekken.e: From Middle Dutch aentrecken. Equivalent to aan (“to, on”) + trekken (“to pull”).

s.def: to be featured, to figure (to be represented in something, to be a part of something)

abondance.e: Borrowed from French abondance, from Latin abundantia.

examples: Het is aannemelijk dat de prijzen zullen stijgen.
De vraag naar het product is sterk afgenomen in de afgelopen maanden.
Door efficiëntere processen is de wachttijd sterk afgenomen voor klanten.
De arts moet het verband afnemen om de wond te controleren.
Hij had zijn hoed afgenomen.
Ze zal een kleine hoeveelheid bloed afnemen voor het onderzoek.
Hij moet volgende week een medische test afnemen om zijn gezondheid te controleren.
De leraar zal aan het eind van het semester een examen afnemen om de kennis van de studenten te evalueren.

afnemen.e: From Middle Dutch afnemen, avenemen, from Old Dutch avaneman, from Proto-Germanic *abanemaną; equivalent to af + nemen.

atmosfeer.e: Borrowed from French atmosphère. Equivalent to atmo- (“air”) + -sfeer (“globe”).

anekdote.e: Borrowed from French anecdote, from Latin anecdota, plural of anecdotum, from Ancient Greek ἀνέκδοτος (anékdotos), from ἀν- (an-, “not, un-”) + ἔκδοτος (ékdotos, “published”).

ångström.e: From Swedish Ångström, the surname of physicist Anders Jonas Ångström.

s.def: to clean/level etc. with a squeegee
to wank, to jerk off, to give a hand job; to stimulate one's own or another person's penis using the hand

aftrekken.e: From Middle Dutch aftrecken. The sense “to subtract” was coined by Simon Stevin as a calque of Latin dētrahō. Equivalent to af + trekken.

examples: Ik was op de Koninklijke Militaire Academie in Bandung, voor twee jaar. Maar het is natuurlijk zo dat men niet op een koninklijke academie kan leren hoe men een leger in een revolutionaire situatie kan opbouwen. Dat heb ik later wel, min of meer via lectuur, een beetje doorgedacht, tijdens de Japanse bezetting.

academie.e: From Middle French académie, from Latin acadēmīa, from Ancient Greek Ἀκαδημία (Akadēmía), a grove of trees and gymnasium outside of Athens where Plato taught; from the name of the supposed former owner of that estate, the Attic hero Akademos. Compare academe, academia, Akademeia.

examples: Ze probeerde de stekker in het stopcontact te steken, maar het paste niet. Toen deed ze het andersom en lukte het wel.
Normaal gesproken loop ik eerst door het park en dan door de winkelstraat, maar vandaag deed ik het andersom om wat tijd te besparen.

afstaan.e: From Middle Dutch afstaen. Equivalent to af + staan.

s.def: delay, retardation; the state of being behind others (e.g. peers)
the condition of being behind one's opponent(s); the score or quantity that forms the deficit

examples: Ik wist niet of ik het al dan niet moest lezen.
Dit is al dan niet een bewuste keuze.

al dan niet.e: In this term, al contrasts niet, synonymously with the more common wel. The term can therefore be roughly literally translated as "yes than not".

acceptabel.e: Borrowed from Middle French acceptable, from Latin acceptābilis. Equivalent to accepteren + -abel.

s.def: antonym (word which has the opposite meaning)

antoniem.e: Borrowed from French antonyme, created to serve as the opposite of synonyme, from Ancient Greek ἀντί (antí, “opposite”) + ὄνομα (ónoma, “name, word”).

aangaande.e: From Middle Dutch aengaande. Compare the Swedish, Norwegian and Danish angående which comes from Low Saxon or Dutch.

s.def: action, activity, movement; especially (without article) a fast-paced activity
examples: De kinderen waren druk bezig met hun speelse acties in het park.
De actiefilm zat vol met spannende acties en spectaculaire stunts.
De natuurkundeleraar demonstreerde de wet van actie en reactie in de klas.
De wet van behoud van actie stelt dat het totale impulsmoment van een systeem constant blijft.
De gerechtelijke acties van de erfgenamen betroffen de verdeling van de actiën in wijlen vaders zaak.

actie.e: From Middle Dutch actie, directly or ultimately from Latin āctiō (“action”).

aangezicht.e: From Middle Dutch aengesichte. Equivalent to aan (“on”) + gezicht (“face, view”).

aureool.e: Borrowed from French auréole, from Latin aureola.

accepteren.e: From Middle Dutch accepteren, from Middle French accepter, from Latin acceptō.

s.def: Intensifying prefix for superlatives: very; of them all
examples: Zijn allergrootste verdriet was dat zijn zoon de zaak niet wilde overnemen.

aller-.e: From Middle Dutch aller-, alder-, the genitive plural of alle (“all”). The prefix thus literally translates to "of all", yielding adjectives such as allerergst (from ergst (“worst”)) which literally translates to "the worst of all". Words formed this way are thus originally univerbations, althoug...

armoede.e: From Middle Dutch armoede, from earlier armode by analogy with moed, from Old Dutch *armodi, from Proto-West Germanic *armōdī, from Proto-Germanic *armōdiją.

examples: De oorzaken van botbreuken zijn onder te verdelen in twee grote categorieën. Enerzijds ten gevolge van grote externe krachten (bijvoorbeeld ongeval) die op het bot uitgeoefend worden en waartegen het bot niet bestand is. Anderzijds kan er een pathologie van het bot aanwezig zijn waardoor het bot zo broos wordt dat het breekt onder invloed van normale krachten zoals wandelen en rechtstaan (bijvoorbeeld bij osteoporose, botmetastase).

anderzijds.e: From ander + zijde + -s, calque of German anderseits.

associatie.e: Borrowed from Middle French association, from Latin associātiō. Equivalent to associëren + -atie.

ambt.e: From Middle Dutch ampt. Originally the same word as ambacht and possibly a mere variant of it. 1765 October 14, letter in De Denker, vol. 3, page 323.

5 Ik haal daar toe de schouders op — terwyl ik met Verontwaardiging aandenk, hoe men, alleen uit eigenbaat of laaghartigheid, het tegenwoordig geslagt benadeelt heeft.

s.def: a subsidy, usually a rebate, for a couple of whom one partner does not participate in the labour market

examples: Gedurende het afschuimen moet de oplossing steeds kokende gehouden worden, waardoor de digtheid van het vocht toeneemt, en het schuim gemakkelijker naar boven stijgt; men zorgt daarbij echter, dat door het te sterk koken, het schuim niet over den rand des ketels loopt.

1853, P. F. Klijnsma, "Het ijzer. Zijne smelting en bewerking", in Algemeen handboek van kunsten en wetenschappen. Nijverheid, vol. 8, in, Volks-bibliotheek, Weijtingh & Brave, 17.
Tusschen de kruin van den dam en het uiteinde der ijzeren plaat, die het onderste gedeelte van het gewelf der werkplaats draagt, bevindt zich het schepgat, waardoor men in den kroes zien, en het noodige werk onder in den oven verrigten kan, zooals het afschuimen van de overtollige vreemde stoffen van het ijzer en het scheppen hiervan met lepels tot het gieten van verschillende voorwerpen in vormen, enz.

1838, Adriaan van der Hoop, Junior, De Renegaat. Een berymd verhaal, Elix & co., 80-81.

Ze schuimden de straten af, kwamen vooral in het donker uit hun schuilplaatsen tevoorschijn.

alsof er een engeltje op je tong piest.i: /ɑlsˌɔf ər ən ˈɛ.ŋəl.tjə ˌɔp jə ˈtɔŋ ˈpist/
alsof er een engeltje op je tong piest.e: First attested in the 1890s in the form ’t is of een engeltje op je tong piest, with een often being replaced by ’n. First used partially in an 1893 story by Jacobus van Looy, who omits piest, then used in full in an 1897 story by Herman Heijermans. Literally, “as if a little angel [e.g. a putto]...

Merkwaardig is dit omdat wij na een grondige studie van de samenleving tot de conclusie kwamen dat er voor Black Power in Suriname niet alleen een plaats is maar dat er boven dien een dringende behoefte aan bestaat. Vooral in kringen van de AFRO SURINAMERS^([sic]) waar de klappen van een eeuwen oude bezetting van het land door de vendien^([sic – meaning bovendien]) een dringende be^([sic – meaning behoefte]) voeld^([sic – meaning gevoeld]).

agnetendal.e: First attested as S. Agneeten dale in 1570. Compound of the genetive form of Agnes (a personal name) and dal (“dale, valley”). Named after Saint Agnes of Rome, the patron of a former monastery which once stood near the current neighbourhood.

s.def: a hamlet in Reusel-De Mierden, North Brabant, Netherlands

achterste heikant.e: First attested as Achterste Heikant in 1894. Derived from achterste (“rearmost”) and a compound of hei (“heath, heathland”) and kant (“side, edge”). So named to distinguish the settlement from Voorste Heikant.

s.def: a hamlet in Best, North Brabant, Netherlands

aarle.e: First attested as aerle in 1496. Compound of Old Dutch *are (“maple, Acer sp.”) and lo (“light forest”). Compare Aarle-Rixtel, Taarlo, Ten Aarlo and Tynaarlo.

s.def: a hamlet in Nuenen, Gerwen en Nederwetten, North Brabant, Netherlands

alvershool.e: First attested as alvenshoel and ten alvensoele in 1455. Compound of alver (“common bleak, Alburnus alburnus”) and hool (“low-lying swampy place”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Eindhoven, North Brabant, Netherlands

achtse molen.e: Derived from the toponym Acht and molen (“mill”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Eindhoven, North Brabant, Netherlands

achtse barrier.e: First attested as Tol in 1866. Derived from the toponym Acht and dialectal barrier (“toll, tollpost”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Eindhoven, North Brabant, Netherlands

aanschot.e: First attested as anscot in 1312. Derived from Middle Dutch aenschot (“common lands next to a settlement”). Compare Overschot and Vrijschot.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Geldrop-Mierlo, North Brabant, Netherlands

akert.e: First attested as bouwplan Akert in 1963. Potentially a hydronym. Alternatively, derived from akker (“farmland”) with emphatic -t.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Laarbeek, North Brabant, Netherlands

aarle-rixtel.e: Aarle is first attested as arleh in 1249. Rixtel is first attested as ricstelle in 1179. Aarle derives from a compound of Old Dutch *are (“maple, Acer sp.”) and lo (“light forest”). Rixtel derives from a compound of an unclear first element and Middle Dutch stelle (“stable, safe haven”). The firs...

s.def: a hamlet in Laarbeek, North Brabant, Netherlands

achterbosch.e: First attested as Achterbosch in 1838-1857. Compound of achter (“back, rear”) and bos (“forest”).

s.def: a hamlet in Meierijstad, North Brabant, Netherlands

achterste hermalen.e: First attested as d'Achterste Herremaelen in 1654. Derived from achterste (“rearmost”) and the toponym Hermalen.

s.def: a neighbourhood of 's-Hertogenbosch, North Brabant, Netherlands

aawijk.e: Compound of the hydronym Aa and wijk (“neighbourhood, quarter”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of Altena, North Brabant, Netherlands

almkerk.e: First attested as almekercke in 1292. Compound of the hydronym Alm (an old arm of the Meuse) and kerk (“church”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of Altena, North Brabant, Netherlands

andel.e: First attested as analo in 850. Compound of Old Dutch ana (“high, upper, elevated”) and lo (“light forest”). Formerly split into a village and a hamlet called Op-Andel and Neer-Andel ("Upstream Andel" and "Downstream Andel").

s.def: a former municipality of North Brabant, Netherlands

aalburg.e: Named after Aalburg, currently part of Wijk en Aalburg.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Tilburg, North Brabant, Netherlands

achthoeven.e: First attested as In de Achthoeven in 1571. Compound of acht (“eight”) and the plural form of hoeve (“piece of land of a certain size”). So named to distinguish the then-hamlet from Zeshoeven.

s.def: a hamlet in Goirle, North Brabant, Netherlands

abcoven.e: First attested as abbichoven in 1380. Compound of Old Dutch Abbe (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ing- and the dative plural form of hof (“farmstead, homestead”).

s.def: a former municipality of North Brabant, Netherlands
a hamlet in Alphen-Chaam, North Brabant, Netherlands

alphen-oosterwijk.e: First attested as tAlfen Oosterwyck in 1540. Compound of the toponym Alphen and a compound of ooster- (“eastern, east-”) + wijk (“village, settlement”).

s.def: a hamlet in Breda, North Brabant, Netherlands

achterste rith.e: Derived from achterste (“rearmost”) and the toponym Rith.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Etten-Leur, North Brabant, Netherlands

attelaken.e: First attested as te attelaken in 1312-1350. Etymology unknown. Originally a hydronym composed of a term that may be related to Etten-Leur and laak (“natural watercourse, drainage channel, bywash”). Formerly an independent hamlet. Compare Barlaque, Eethen and Etten.

s.def: a village in Zundert, North Brabant, Netherlands

achtmaal.e: First attested as achtmale in 1294. Compound of Middle Dutch achte (“preserve, lordly property, legal district”) and maal (“place where law is spoken”). Compare Acht, Achterveld and Echt.

s.def: a hamlet in Moerdijk, North Brabant, Netherlands
a hamlet in Vijfheerenlanden, Utrecht, Netherlands

achterdijk.e:
* (Noord-Brabant) Attested as Achterdijk in 1899. Derived from achterdijk (“inland levee”).
* (Utrecht) Attested as Achter-Dijk in 1869. Derived from achterdijk (“inland levee”). Named after a nearby levee.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Bergen op Zoom, North Brabant, Netherlands

augustapolder.e: First attested as Augusta Pder in 1838-1857. Compound of Augusta (a personal name) and polder (“polder”). Named after Elisabeth Maria Aloysa Augusta of the Pfalz (1721-1794), the wife of then-Marquis of Bergen op Zoom, Charles Theodore of Sulzbach (1729-1795).

s.def: a village in Tholen, Zeeland, Netherlands

anna jacobapolder.e:
Attested as Anna Jacobapolder in 1838-1857. Compound of Anna (a personal name) and Jacoba (a personal name) and polder (“polder”). Named after the wife of the initiator of the land reclamation project which resulted in the creation of the polder.
See also Zealandic Annajacobapolder.

examples: Het is daarom te hopen dat de delegatie goed geluisterd heeft naar de adviezen die ze ongetwijfeld aan de Noordzee heeft gekregen.
De beste stuurlui staan op dit moment glashelder aan wal en lopen vaak gewoon te raaskallen, zoals die meneer die ineens denkt hulp te moeten gaan zoeken bij bakra basi aan de Noordzee.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Veere, Zeeland, Netherlands

aagtekerke.e:
First attested as in atrio Sanctae Agathae in 1327. Compound of the name of Saint Agatha and kerk (“church”).
See also Zealandic Aegte, 't Aflat.

s.def: a city and former municipality of Middelburg, Zeeland, Netherlands

arnemuiden.e:
First attested as arnemuda in 1233. Compound of the hydronym Arne and the dative plural form of muide (“mouth”). The hydronym Arne either derives from Proto-Indo-European *er- (“move”) or from an older form Rinne through metathesis.
See also Zealandic Erremu, Erremuje.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Middelburg, Zeeland, Netherlands

arnestein.e: First attested as Arnestein in the 18th century. From the hydronym Arne + -stein (“stone building, castle, manor”). Named after an estate.

s.def: a hamlet in Goes, Zeeland, Netherlands

abbekinderen.e:
First attested as Abbekinderen in 1571. Compound of Middle Dutch Abbe (a personal name) and the plural form of kind (“child”). The toponym refers to an undivided property inherited by the children of an otherwise unknown individual Abbe. Compare 's-Heer Hendrikskinderen and Waanskinderen.
See als...

s.def: a hamlet in Hulst, Zeeland, Netherlands

absdale.e: First attested as te absdale around 1315. Compound of abt (“abbot”) and dal (“dale, valley”).

s.def: a city and former municipality of Sluis, Zeeland, Netherlands

aardenburg.e: First attested as rodenburgh in 966. Compound of an unclear first element and burg (“fortified settlement, castle”). The first element may be a Celtic hydronym *Rodana, rood (“red”), or a term derived from the Latin governmental designation pagus Rodanis. The first element was gradually reinterpr...

s.def: a hamlet in Sluis, Zeeland, Netherlands

akkerput.e: Attested as Akkerput in 1899. Compound of akker (“farmland”) and put (“well, storage pit for vegetables”).

s.def: to take care of, to nurse

achterwaren.e: From Middle Dutch achterwaren. Equivalent to achter- + waren (“to watch, keep, preserve”).

s.def: to tear away, snatch (away), pluck off
a neighbourhood of Almere, Flevoland, Netherlands

almere haven.e: Attested as Almerehaven in 1976. Derived from the toponym Almere and haven (“harbour”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Almere, Flevoland, Netherlands

almere stad.e: Derived from the toponym Almere and stad (“city”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Almere, Flevoland, Netherlands

almere buiten.e: Derived from the toponym Almere and buiten (“outside, outer-”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Almere, Flevoland, Netherlands

almere poort.e: Derived from the toponym Almere and poort (“gate, gateway”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Almere, Flevoland, Netherlands

almere hout.e: Derived from the toponym Almere and hout (“rising forest”). Named after a nearby forest range.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Almere, Flevoland, Netherlands

almere pampus.e: Derived from the toponyms Almere and Pampus.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Lelystad, Flevoland, Netherlands

atolwijk.e: Compound of atol (“atoll”) and wijk (“neighbourhood, quarter”). The street names in the neighbourhood reference various marine landforms.

s.def: a hamlet in Zwartewaterland, Overijssel, Netherlands

afsched.e: Potentially attested as ascet in 1302, attested with certainty as AFSCHED of Asched, ook wel Afzet in 1839. If the oldest attestations refer to the current settlement, the toponym is a derivation from Old Dutch askethe (“ash (Fraxinus excelsior) forest”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Zwolle, Overijssel, Netherlands

aa-landen.e: Attested as Aa landen in 1830-1855. Derived from the hydronym Aa and the plural form of land (“land”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Zwolle, Overijssel, Netherlands

assendorp.e:
Potentially attested as aschendorpe in pago isloi in the middle of the 11th century, attested with certainty as van aschendorpe in 1403. Compound of Old Dutch Asko (a personal name) and dorp (“farmstead, settlement”). Formerly an independent hamlet.
See also Dutch Low Saxon Assendörp.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Dalfsen, Overijssel, Netherlands

ankummer es.e: Derived from the toponym Ankum and es (“farmland next to a village”).

s.def: a hamlet in Dalfsen, Overijssel, Netherlands

ankum.e: First attested as to anken in 1381-1383. Etymology unknown. Not derived from a term related to Middle Dutch eng (“farmland next to a village”), a compound of a personal name and heem (“home, settlement”) or dialectal ank (“pool, kolk lake”). The toponym bears a passing resemblance to Old High Ger...

s.def: a hamlet in Ommen, Overijssel, Netherlands

archem.e: First attested as arachem in 947. Compound of either Old Dutch Arho (a personal name) or Proto-Germanic *arga- (“bad, worthless”) and heem (“home, settlement”). Compare Arkel.

s.def: a hamlet in Ommen, Overijssel, Netherlands

arriën.e: First attested as de arien in 1294-1295. Potentially derived from a term cognate to Middle Dutch eriën (“plough, till”).

s.def: a hamlet in Ommen, Overijssel, Netherlands

arriërveld.e: First attested as Arriën Veld in 1867. Compound of the toponym Arriën and veld (“field”).

s.def: a hamlet in Hardenberg, Overijssel, Netherlands

anerveen.e: First attested as Anerveen in 1846. Compound of the toponym Ane and veen (“peat bog”).

s.def: a hamlet in Hardenberg, Overijssel, Netherlands

anevelde.e: First attested as anewede in 1259. Compound of the toponym Ane and veld (“field”). The second element was initially Middle Dutch wede (“pasture”).

s.def: a former municipality of Overijssel, Netherlands

avereest.e: First attested as in resta in 1217. Named after a toponym derived from a compound of over (“on the other side of”) and the hydronym Reest.

s.def: a hamlet in Deventer, Overijssel, Netherlands

apenhuizen.e: First attested as apenhuizen in 1461. Compound of Old Dutch Apo (a personal name) and the dative plural form of huis (“home”). Compare Eppenhuizen and Appenheim.

s.def: a hamlet in Deventer, Overijssel, Netherlands

averlo.e: First attested as varendeloe around 1300. Compound of Proto-Germanic *farnôþu- (“place of ferns”) and lo (“light forest”). The first element was gradually reinterpreted to a form of over (“on the other side of”).

s.def: a hamlet in Hof van Twente, Overijssel, Netherlands

azelo.e: First attested as aselo in 1333. Likely a compound of Old Dutch Aso (a personal name) and lo (“light forest”).

s.def: a village in Almelo, Overijssel, Netherlands

aadorp.e: First attested as Aabrug in 1867. Compound of the hydronym Aa and dorp (“village, settlement”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Almelo, Overijssel, Netherlands

aalderinkshoek.e: Attested as Aalderinkshoek in 1867. Compound of Middle Dutch Aalder (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ing- and hoek (“isolated place, sector”).

s.def: a village in Tubbergen, Overijssel, Netherlands

albergen.e: First attested as in alberge in the late 10th century. Compound of either Old Dutch alf (“elf, supernatural creature”) or alf (“white”) and berg (“hill, elevated place”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Enschede, Overijssel, Netherlands

amelink.e: First attested as amelinchoff in 1385. Derived from Middle Dutch Amel (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ing-.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Hengelo, Overijssel, Netherlands

anninks- en nijhofshoek.e: First attested as anning in 1475. Derived from a toponym derived from Old Dutch Ano (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ing- and a second toponym derived from a compound of nij (“new”) and hof (“farmstead, homestead”). The two toponyms are further suffixed with hoek (“isola...

s.def: a former municipality of Overijssel, Netherlands

ambt delden.e: Derived from ambt (“type of legal district, bailiwick”) and Delden.

s.def: a former municipality of Overijssel, Netherlands

ambt almelo.e: Derived from ambt (“type of legal district, bailiwick”) and Almelo.

s.def: a former municipality of Overijssel, Netherlands

ambt hardenberg.e: Derived from ambt (“type of legal district, bailiwick”) and Hardenberg.

s.def: a former municipality of Overijssel, Netherlands

ambt ommen.e: Derived from ambt (“type of legal district, bailiwick”) and Ommen.

s.def: a former municipality of Overijssel, Netherlands

ambt vollenhove.e: Derived from ambt (“type of legal district, bailiwick”) and Vollenhove.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Tilburg, North Brabant, Netherlands

armhoef.e: Compound of arm (“poor”) and hoef (“farmstead, homestead”). The neighbourhood was formerly the location of two farmsteads owned by a poor relief organisation.

s.def: a neighbourhood of 's-Hertogenbosch, North Brabant, Netherlands

abdijenbuurt.e: Compound of the plural form of abdij (“abbey”) and buurt (“neighbourhood, quarter”). The neighbourhood's streets are named after famous abbeys.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Helmond, North Brabant, Netherlands

annabuurt.e: Compound of Anna (a personal name) and buurt (“neighbourhood, quarter”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Arnhem, Gelderland, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Beuningen, Gelderland, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Wijchen, Gelderland, Netherlands
a village in De Wolden, Drenthe, Netherlands

ansen.e: First attested as anze and enze around 1232. Etymology unknown. Compare Ens.

s.def: a hamlet in De Wolden, Drenthe, Netherlands

anholt.e: First attested as Anhold in 1811-1813. Derived from Dutch Low Saxon aanhold (“stopover, safe resting area”). Possibly derived from the name of an old posthouse.

s.def: a hamlet in De Wolden, Drenthe, Netherlands

armweide.e: First attested as narberingeweide in 1407. Derived from a contracted compound of Middle Dutch Narbert (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ing- and weide (“pasture”).

s.def: a village in Coevorden, Drenthe, Netherlands

aalden.e: First attested as alede in 1332. Etymology uncertain. Potentially derived from an ancient tree or plant name (compare Old Norse alað (“food, meal”), aldin (“edible fruit of a tree”), Old Swedish alda (“fruit-bearing oak”)). Compare Aalten and Eelde.

s.def: a hamlet in Coevorden, Drenthe, Netherlands

achterste erm.e: First attested as Achterste-Erm in 1936. Derived from achterste (“rearmost”) and the toponym Erm.

s.def: a hamlet in Emmen, Drenthe, Netherlands

amsterdamscheveld.e: First attested as Het Amsterdamsche Veld in 1851-1855. Compound of the toponym Amsterdam and veld (“field”). The settlement was financed by a group of entrepreneurs from Amsterdam.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Emmen, Drenthe, Netherlands

angelslo.e: First attested as angel around 1325. Compound of either Proto-Germanic *angula (“corner”) or Middle Dutch angel (“fishhook, fishing rod”) and lo (“small pool”). Formerly an independent hamlet.

s.def: a hamlet in Midden-Groningen, Groningen, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Emmen, Drenthe, Netherlands

achterdiep.e:
* (Groningen) Attested as t Agter Diep in 1781. Compound of achter (“back, rearward”) and diep (“canal”), here referring to a specific type of drainage canal. Named after a nearby canal. See also Dutch Low Saxon Aachterdaip.
* (Drenthe) Attested as Achterdiep in 1899. Compound of achter (“back, r...

s.def: a village and former municipality of Aa en Hunze, Drenthe, Netherlands

anloo.e:
First attested as anloe in 1139. Compound of Middle Dutch an (“up, high”) and lo (“light forest”). Compare Anreep.
See also Dutch Low Saxon Anlo.

s.def: a hamlet in Aa en Hunze, Drenthe, Netherlands

achter 't hout.e: First attested as achter 't Holt around 1900. Derived from achter (“behind”) and hout (“rising deciduous forest”).

s.def: a hamlet in Assen, Drenthe, Netherlands

anreep.e: Potentially attested as anrep in 1141, attested with certainty as in anrepe in 1217. Compound of Middle Dutch an (“on, up”) and a variant of rijp (“strip of land, waterside, edge”). Compare Anloo.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Assen, Drenthe, Netherlands

amelterhout.e: Compound of the toponym Amelte and hout (“rising deciduous forest”).

s.def: a hamlet in Groningen, Groningen, Netherlands

achter-thesinge.e:
First attested as Achter-Thesing in 1840. Compound of achter (“back, backward”) and the toponym Thesinge.
See also Dutch Low Saxon Taisner Hammerk.

s.def: a hamlet in Westerwolde, Groningen, Netherlands

abeltjeshuis.e:
First attested as Abeldjes huis in 1794. Compound of a diminutive form of Abel (a personal name) and huis (“house”). Originally the name of a farmhouse which also served as a local inn.
See also Dutch Low Saxon Oabeltjeshuus.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Westerwolde, Groningen, Netherlands

agodorp.e:
Compound of the name of the AGO wood processing company and dorp (“village, settlement”). Compare Batadorp, Heveadorp and Philipsdorp.
See also Dutch Low Saxon Agodörp.

s.def: a hamlet in Eemsdelta, Groningen, Netherlands
a hamlet in Het Hogeland, Groningen, Netherlands

arwerd.e:
* (Eemsdelta) First attested as arnuurd in the 10th or 11th century. Compound of either Old Frisian ern (“eagle”) or Aro (a personal name) and uurd (“terp, artificial mound for habitation”). Compare Krewerd.
* (Het Hogeland) See also Dutch Low Saxon Aarwerd.

s.def: a hamlet in Eemsdelta, Groningen, Netherlands

amsweer.e: First attested as damsweer in 1447. Compound of an unclear first element and Old Frisian weer (“artificial mound used for habitation”). The first element may be Old Frisian Amze (a personal name) or Middle Dutch ambt (“legal district”), referring here to the Oldambt.

s.def: a hamlet in Eemsdelta, Groningen, Netherlands

andere wereld.e: First attested as De Andere Wereld in 1828. Derived from ander (“other”) and wereld (“world”). Originally the name of a house. The toponym may refer to the remoteness of the hamlet.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Het Hogeland, Groningen, Netherlands

adorp.e:
First attested as artharpe in 1371. Etymology unknown. Compound of an unclear first element and dorp (“village, settlement”). Proposed etymologies for the first element include Old Frisian arth (“farmland”) and arn (“eagle”).
See also Dutch Low Saxon Oadörp.

s.def: a hamlet in Het Hogeland, Groningen, Netherlands

abbeweer.e: First attested as opp abba wera medum in 1471. Compound of Old Frisian Abbe (a personal name) and weer (“artificial mound used for habitation”).

s.def: a hamlet in Het Hogeland, Groningen, Netherlands

alinghuizen.e:
First attested as aldynghehusen in 1410. Compound of Old Frisian Ale (a personal name) suffixed with -ingi- and the plural form of huis (“house”).
See also Dutch Low Saxon Allenhoezen.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Westerkwartier, Groningen, Netherlands

aduard.e:
First attested as adawerth in 1252. Compound of Old Frisian Ada (a personal name) or Ade (a personal name) and werth (“terp, artificial mound used for habitation”).
See also Dutch Low Saxon Auwerd.

s.def: a village in Noardeast-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands
a hamlet in Westerkwartier, Groningen, Netherlands

aalsum.e:
* (Friesland) First attested as atlesheim in 944. Borrowed from West Frisian Ealsum, derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian Athal (a personal name) and hem (“home, settlement”).
* (Groningen) First attested as aelsum in 1449. Difficult to interpret due to a paucity of old attestations. Po...

s.def: a hamlet in Westerkwartier, Groningen, Netherlands

aduarder voorwerk.e: First attested as van den convente of van den vorwarken van adewart in 1389. Derived from the toponym Aduard and voorwerk (“monastic grange”).

s.def: a hamlet in Westerkwartier, Groningen, Netherlands

aduarderzijl.e:
First attested as van den zyle, de adewerder ziil gheheten is in 1382. Compound of the toponym Aduard and zijl (“discharge sluice”).
See also Dutch Low Saxon Auwerderziel.

s.def: a hamlet in Westerkwartier, Groningen, Netherlands

allersma.e: First attested as Duert Allersma als een rechter toe Edsinge in 1504. Derived from West Frisian Allersma (a surname). Originally the name of a manor, which was named in turn after the family which owned it.

s.def: a hamlet in Westerkwartier, Groningen, Netherlands

antum.e: See also Dutch Low Saxon Antam.

s.def: a former municipality of Friesland, Netherlands
a village and former municipality of Achtkarspelen, Friesland, Netherlands

augustinusga.e: First attested as parrochia beati Augustini around 1240. Calque of West Frisian Stynsgea, a compound of the name of Saint Augustine and ga (“village, village area”).

s.def: a village in Noardeast-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

augsbuurt.e: First attested as aeltsbuur in the 15th century. Potentially a compound of Old Frisian Alder (a personal name) and buur (“house, cottage”). The Dutch toponym is derived from an older Frisian name for the settlement: its current (official) Frisian name is Lytsewâld.

s.def: a hamlet in Noardeast-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

abbewier.e: First attested as Tzijaewijer in 1511. Compound of West Frisian Abbe (a personal name) and wier (“artificial mound used for habitation”). The official Frisian name of the settlement derives from an older name of the hamlet.

s.def: a hamlet in Leeuwarden, Friesland, Netherlands

abbengawier.e: First attested as abbingweer in 1448. Derived from a compound of Old Frisian Abbinga (a surname) and wier (“artificial mound used for habitation”). The Dutch form of the toponym was borrowed from a significantly older version of the Frisian name.

s.def: a hamlet in Leeuwarden, Friesland, Netherlands

angwier.e: First attested as Angweer in 1543. Borrowed from West Frisian Angwier, derived in turn from a compound of an unclear first element and Old Frisian wier (“artificial mound used for habitation”). Compare Engwier.

s.def: a village in Leeuwarden, Friesland, Netherlands

aegum.e: First attested as agum in 1503. Borrowed from West Frisian Eagum, potentially derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian Age (a personal name) and hēm (“home, settlement”).

s.def: a hamlet in Opsterland, Friesland, Netherlands

allardsoog.e: First attested as Allartsoog in 1851. Compound of Allard (a personal name) and oog (“eye”). Originally the name of an inn. The inn may have been named after Allart Scheltinga, a nobleman who owned lands near the current hamlet.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Heerenveen, Friesland, Netherlands

akkrum.e: First attested as ackrom in 1315. Borrowed from West Frisian Akkrum, of unknown etymology. Potentially derived from Old Frisian ake (“fish weir”). Various proposed compounds of a personal name or oikonym and hēm (“home, settlement”) do not match the oldest attestations of the toponym.

s.def: a hamlet in Heerenveen, Friesland, Netherlands
a hamlet in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

anneburen.e:
* (Heerenveen) Calque of West Frisian Annebuorren, a compound of Anne (a personal name) and the plural form of buer (“house, cottage”). The hamlet was named after Anne Seizes van der Kerk, who owned a farm in the settlement in the 19th century.
* (Súdwest-Fryslân) First attested as Anne Buiren in...

s.def: a village in Ooststellingwerf, Friesland, Netherlands

appelscha.e: First attested as appels in 1247 and 1251. Borrowed from West Frisian Appelskea, potentially derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian appel (“apple”) and *skea (“forest”); however, the oldest attestations do not support this etymology.

s.def: a hamlet in Ooststellingwerf, Friesland, Netherlands

aekinga.e: First attested as Aeckinga in the 17th century. Derived from West Frisian Aekinga (a surname).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Leeuwarden, Friesland, Netherlands

achter de hoven.e: Derived from achter (“behind”) and the plural form of hof (“garden”). The neighbourhood is located on an area which formerly housed communal gardens.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Leeuwarden, Friesland, Netherlands

aldlân.e: Borrowed from West Frisian Aldlân, a compound of âld (“old”) and lân (“land”). The toponym contrasts with Nijlân.

s.def: a village in De Fryske Marren, Friesland, Netherlands

akmarijp.e: First attested as ackrommarijp in 1315. Derived from a compound of the toponym Akkrum and Old Frisian ripe (“edge, bank”).

s.def: a village in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

abbega.e: First attested as abbahem in 1250-1275. Borrowed from West Frisian Abbegea, derived from a compound of Old Frisian Abbe (a personal name) and gâ (“village, village area”). The second element was initially hēm (“home, settlement”).

s.def: a village in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

allingawier.e: First attested as alingwere around 1275. Borrowed from West Frisian Allingawier, derived from a compound of Old Frisian Ale (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ingi- and wier (“artificial mound used for habitation”).

s.def: a hamlet in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

aaksens.e: First attested as Aaxens in 1718. Borrowed from West Frisian Aaksens, likely derived in turn from Old Frisian Aesga (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ingi-.

s.def: a hamlet in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

abbegaasterketting.e: First attested as Abbegaster Ketting Huys in 1718. Calque of West Frisian Abbegeaster Ketting, derived from the toponym Abbegea and ketting (“chain”). The toponym refers to a chain drawn across a nearby river as part of a toll post.

s.def: a hamlet in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

abbegaasterrige.e: First attested as Abbegatster Rijge in 1853. Calque of West Frisian Abbegeaster Rige, derived from the toponym Abbegea and rige (“row of houses, embankment”).

s.def: a hamlet in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

andelahuizen.e: First attested as Andlahuysen in 1543. Calque of West Frisian Andelahuzen, a compound of Andela (a surname) and the plural form of hûs (“house”).

s.def: a hamlet in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

angterp.e: First attested as aucterp in 1543. Borrowed from West Frisian Eangterp, potentially derived from a compound of Old Frisian Andger (a personal name) and therp (“artificial mound used for habitation”).

s.def: a hamlet in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

arkum.e: First attested as Arcum in 1664. Borrowed from West Frisian Arkum. Difficult to interpret due to a paucity of old attestations. Potentially derived from a compound of Old Frisian Arke (a personal name) or Adrik and hēm (“home, settlement”).

s.def: a hamlet in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

atzeburen.e: First attested as aetzabuyren in 1478. Calque of West Frisian Atsebuorren, derived from a compound of Old Frisian Atze (a personal name) and the plural form of buer (“house, cottage”).

s.def: a village in Waadhoeke, Friesland, Netherlands

achlum.e: First attested as echtlum in 1319. Borrowed from West Frisian Achlum, of uncertain etymology. Potentially derived from a compound of Old Frisian Achtele (a personal name) and hēm (“home, settlement”). Compare Echten.

s.def: a hamlet in Waadhoeke, Friesland, Netherlands

arkens.e: First attested as erdenze [read: erckenze] in 1433. Derived from a compound of Old Frisian Arke (a personal name) suffixed with -ingi-. The Dutch form of the toponym was borrowed from an earlier version of the Frisian name.

s.def: a hamlet and former municipality of Harlingen, Friesland, Netherlands

almenum.e: First attested as almenum in 1442. Borrowed from West Frisian Almenum, of unknown etymology. Not derived from a personal name.

s.def: a village in Goeree-Overflakkee, South Holland, Netherlands
a hamlet in Molenlanden, South Holland, Netherlands

achthuizen.e:
* (Goeree-Overflakkee) Attested as De Agthuisen around 1750. Compound of acht (“eight”) and the plural form of huis (“house”). See also Zealandic Achthuûzen.
* (Molenlanden) Compound of acht (“eight”) and the plural form of huis (“house”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of Nissewaard, South Holland, Netherlands

abbenbroek.e: First attested as appenbruech in 1206. Compound of Middle Dutch Abbe (a personal name) and broek (“marsh, marshland”).

s.def: a hamlet in Zwijndrecht, South Holland, Netherlands

achter-lindt.e: Attested as Achter-Lindt in 1851. Compound of achter (“back, rear”) and a toponym potentially derived from a hydronym (derived in turn from Proto-Germanic *linþja- (“flow slowly, flow softly”) or, alternatively, from a term cognate to Old High German lentî (“landing”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of Molenlanden, South Holland, Netherlands

arkel.e: First attested as arclo in 999. Etymology uncertain. Potentially a compound of Proto-Germanic *arga- (“bad, worthless”) or Old Dutch Arho (a personal name) and lo (“light forest”). Compare Archem.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Krimpenerwaard, South Holland, Netherlands

ammerstol.e: First attested as in theloneo de ambers in 1221. Compound of the hydronym Ammers (potentially derived from the prehistoric hydronym *Ambarussa) and Middle Dutch tol (“tollhouse”).

s.def: a hamlet in Delft, South Holland, Netherlands

abtswoude.e: First attested as poptesuuolde in 1206-1215. Originally a compound of Old Dutch Popt (a personal name) and walt (“marsh forest, swamp forest”). The first element was changed to Middle Dutch abbet (“abbot”) after Egmond Abbey was granted land near the hamlet.

s.def: a former municipality of Delft, South Holland, Netherlands
a former municipality of South Holland, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Delft, South Holland, Netherlands

afrikabuurt.e: Compound of the toponym Afrika and buurt (“neighbourhood, quarter”). The neighbourhood's streets are named after African countries.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Delft, South Holland, Netherlands

aziëbuurt.e: Compound of the toponym Azië (“Asia”) and buurt (“neighbourhood, quarter”). The neighbourhood's streets are named after Asian countries.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Rijswijk, South Holland, Netherlands

artiestenbuurt.e: Compound of the plural form of artiest (“artist”) and buurt (“neighbourhood, quarter”). The neighbourhood's streets are named after famous actors.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Rotterdam, South Holland, Netherlands

afrikaanderwijk.e: Compound of Afrikaander (“Boer”) and wijk (“neighbourhood, quarter”). The neighbourhood's streets are named after figures from the Boer Wars.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Rotterdam, South Holland, Netherlands

agniesebuurt.e: Compound of the name of Agniese Bokel (d. around 1335) and buurt (“neighbourhood, quarter”). The neighbourhood was named after a noblewoman who owned the land the current neighbourhood stands on.

s.def: a neighbourhood of The Hague, South Holland, Netherlands

arendsdorp.e: Attested as Dorpenwoning before 1648. Named after Arend van Dorp (around 1530-1600), a nobleman who purchased a manor near the current neighbourhood in 1586.

s.def: a neighbourhood of The Hague, South Holland, Netherlands

archipelbuurt.e: Compound of archipel (“archipelago”) and buurt (“neighbourhood, quarter”). The neighbourhood's streets are named after islands of the Indonesian Archipelago.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Voorschoten, South Holland, Netherlands

adegeest.e: Attested as Noort-Hofflants off Adegeester polder in 1647. Potentially a compound of a personal name or the hydronym Ade (derived from a hypercorrection of aa (“water, river”)) and geest (“elevated sandy land”). Named after a former manor.

s.def: a hamlet and former municipality of Montfoort, Utrecht, Netherlands
a hamlet in Vijfheerenlanden, Utrecht, Netherlands
a hamlet in Leiderdorp, South Holland, Netherlands

achthoven.e:
* (Montfoort) First attested as achtehoeven in 1306. Compound of Middle Dutch achte (“eight”) and the plural form of hoeve (“piece of land of a certain size”).
* (Vijfheerenlanden) First attested as cortachoven in the middle of the 14th century. Compound of Middle Dutch achte (“eight”) and the pl...

s.def: a neighbourhood of Leiden, South Holland, Netherlands

academiewijk.e: Compound of academie (“academy”) and wijk (“neighbourhood, quarter”). The neighbourhood houses several faculty buildings of Leiden University.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Alphen aan den Rijn, South Holland, Netherlands

aarlanderveen.e: First attested as arlederuene in 1214. Compound of the hydronym Aar (for which see Ter Aar), Middle Dutch lede (“excavated or canalised watercourse”) and vene (“peat bog, marsh”). The second element was gradually reinterpreted to land (“land”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Alphen aan den Rijn, South Holland, Netherlands

ambachtenbuurt.e: Compound of the plural form of ambacht (“trade”) and buurt (“neighbourhood, quarter”). The neighbourhood's streets are named after traditional trades.

s.def: Abyssinia (former name of Ethiopia: a country and former empire in East Africa; used as an exonym until the mid 20th century)
a hamlet in Bodegraven-Reeuwijk, South Holland, Netherlands

abessinië.e: Named after the country. The hamlet was probably named for its distance from its neighbours.

s.def: a hamlet and former municipality of De Bilt, Utrecht, Netherlands
a hamlet and former municipality of Nieuwkoop, South Holland, Netherlands

achttienhoven.e:
* (Utrecht) First attested as achtienhove in 1287. Compound of Middle Dutch achtien (“eighteen”) and the plural form of hoeve (“plot of land of a certain size”).
* (Zuid-Holland) First attested as achtienhoeven in 1280-1287. Compound of Middle Dutch achtien (“eighteen”) and the plural form of hoe...

s.def: a former municipality of South Holland, Netherlands

alkemade.e: First attested as alkmade in 1182-1206. Compound of Old Dutch *alke (“mud”) or alk (“crow”) and made (“meadow”). The municipality was named after the van Alkemade family, which received its name from the territory it ruled. Compare Alkmaar.

amerikanenkamp.e: Compound of the plural form of Amerikaan (“American”) and kamp (“encampment”). The area was used as a military base by American soldiers guarding the island's oil refinery during the Second World War.

abrahamsz.e: Likely derived from the surname of the first owner of the plantation which the neighbourhood developed around.

antriol.e: Borrowed from Papiamentu Antriol, derived from a contraction of Spanish al (“to”) and interior (“interior”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of Wijdemeren, North Holland, Netherlands

ankeveen.e: First attested as tankenveen in 1344. Compound of Middle Dutch Tanke (a personal name) and veen (“peat bog, marsh”). The initial T- gradually came to be seen as the preposition te (“at”) and was dropped.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Weesp, North Holland, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Amsterdam, North Holland, Netherlands

apollobuurt.e: Compound of the name Apollo and buurt (“neighbourhood, quarter”). The neighbourhood's streets are named after figures and places of Ancient Greece.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Amsterdam, North Holland, Netherlands

admiralenbuurt.e: Compound of the plural form of admiraal (“admiral”) and buurt (“neighbourhood, quarter”). The neighbourhood's streets are named after famous admirals.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Amsterdam, North Holland, Netherlands

amsteldorp.e: Compound of the hydronym Amstel and dorp (“village”).

s.def: a hamlet in Oostzaan, North Holland, Netherlands
a hamlet in Waterland, North Holland, Netherlands
a hamlet in Edam-Volendam, North Holland, Netherlands

axwijk.e: First attested as havicswijk in 1342. Compound of Middle Dutch Havic (a personal name) and wyc (“farmstead, village, settlement”).

s.def: a hamlet in Purmerend, North Holland, Netherlands
a village and former municipality of Zaanstad, North Holland, Netherlands

assendelft.e: First attested as asmedelf in the first half of the 11th century. Compound of Old Dutch askman (“Norman”) and delf (“dug or canalised watercourse”). According to an alternative reading, the first element is Old Frisian *Askman (a personal name) or *Askema (a surname) or Askmanna.

s.def: a village in Haarlemmermeer, North Holland, Netherlands

abbenes.e: Attested as Abbenes in 1867. Named after the former island of Abbenes, a compound of Middle Dutch Abe (a personal name) and nesse (“headland, spit”).

s.def: a hamlet in Haarlemmermeer, North Holland, Netherlands

aalsmeerderbrug.e: Attested as Aalsmeerderbuurt in 1936. Compound of the toponym Aalsmeer and brug (“bridge”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Haarlem, North Holland, Netherlands

amsterdamsewijk.e: Compound of the toponym Amsterdam and wijk (“neighbourhood, quarter”).

aanvalsgeweer.e: From aanval + -s- + geweer. Early attestations suggest a calque from either English assault rifle or French fusil d'assaut.

s.def: a village in Bloemendaal, North Holland, Netherlands

aerdenhout.e: First attested as Aerden-hout in 1517-1524. Compound of an unclear first element and hout (“rising deciduous forest”). A local etymology holds that the first element is derived from ander (“other”) (as opposed to Haarlemmerhout), but there are no old attestations which confirm this derivation.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Heemskerk, North Holland, Netherlands

assumburg.e: First attested as assenburg in 1465. Compound of the toponym Assum and Middle Dutch borch (“castle”). Named after a castle.

s.def: a hamlet in Uitgeest, North Holland, Netherlands

assum.e: First attested as assem in 1397. Likely a compound of Old Dutch *ask (“ash, Fraxinus excelsior”) and hēm (“home, settlement”). According to an alternative hypothesis, the toponym is a contracted compound of Old Dutch askman (“pirate”) and hēm.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Castricum, North Holland, Netherlands

akersloot.e: First attested as ekerslato in 1083. Compound of an unclear first element and Old Frisian slato (“ditch”). The first element may potentially derive from a (variant of) Old Frisian ekker (“farmland”). The first element is unlikely to derive from a personal name or from aker (“acorn”).

s.def: a village in Bergen, North Holland, Netherlands

aagtdorp.e: First attested as ekthorp in 1215-1226. Potentially a compound of Middle Dutch eggh (“corner, boundary”) and dorp (“farmstead, village, secondary settlement”). The first element is unlikely to be a personal name or dialectal aagt (“low-lying pasture”). Compare Bregtdorp.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Koggenland, North Holland, Netherlands

avenhorn.e: First attested as lutekedrecht around 1312. Compound of Middle Dutch Ave (a personal name) and horn (“corner, protruding bend”). The village was originally called Lutekedrecht, a compound of Middle Dutch luttik (“small”) and drecht (“ferry, watercourse”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of Medemblik, North Holland, Netherlands

abbekerk.e: First attested as abbenkerke around 1312. Compound of Middle Dutch Abbe (a personal name) and kerk (“church”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of Medemblik, North Holland, Netherlands

andijk.e: First attested as alhier onder de dijk in 1667. Likely derived from aandijk (“small levee located at the outer foot of a marine or river levee”).

s.def: a village in Opmeer, North Holland, Netherlands

aartswoud.e: First attested as nedartswoude around 1312. Compound of Middle Dutch Edaert (a personal name) and wout (“marsh forest, swamp forest”).

s.def: a hamlet in Opmeer, North Holland, Netherlands

abbekerkeweere.e: Compound of the toponym Abbekerk and were (“piece of land located between two drainage ditches”). Named after a section of a polder.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Hollands Kroon, North Holland, Netherlands

anna paulowna.e: As a location name, first attested as den Nieuwe-Anna-Paulowna-polder in 1846. Named after Anna Pavlovna of Russia.

s.def: a hamlet in Schagen, North Holland, Netherlands

abbestede.e: First attested as Abstee around 1563. Compound of Abbe (a personal name) and stede (“place”). Compare Abstede.

s.def: a village in Aa en Hunze, Drenthe, Netherlands

annerveenschekanaal.e:
First attested as Annerveensche Heeren Compagnie in 1792. Compound of the toponym Annerveen and kanaal (“canal”). Named after a nearby canal.
See also Dutch Low Saxon Annerveensekenaol.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Baarn, Utrecht, Netherlands

amaliapark.e: Compound of Amalia (a personal name) and park (“park”). Named after a public garden named in memory of Princess Amalia of Saxe-Weimar-Eisenach.

s.def: a hamlet in De Bilt, Utrecht, Netherlands

achterwetering.e: Attested as De Achter Weteringe in 1696. Compound of achter (“back, rear-”) and wetering (“drainage channel”). Named after a nearby channel.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Amersfoort, Utrecht, Netherlands

architectenbuurt.e: Compound of the plural form of architect (“architect”) and buurt (“neighbourhood, quarter”). The neighbourhood's streets are named after famous architects.

s.def: a village in Leusden, Utrecht, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of IJsselstein, Utrecht, Netherlands

achterveld.e:
* (Leusden) First attested as achtevelde in 1309. Compound of Middle Dutch achte (“forest preserve, lordly possession, legal districht”) and velt (“field”). Compare Acht, Achtmaal and Echt.
* (IJsselstein) Compound of achter (“back, behind”) and veld (“field”).

s.def: a hamlet in Leusden, Utrecht, Netherlands

asschat.e:
First attested as aschet in 1378-1379. Etymology unknown. Proposed etymologies include a derivation from an unknown toponym *Asch and Middle Dutch gate (“street, way”) and a derivation from asgat (“ashpit”).
Compare Eschede.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Leusden, Utrecht, Netherlands

akkerhoeve.e: Compound of akker (“farmland”) and hoeve (“farmstead”). Named after a former farmhouse.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Leusden, Utrecht, Netherlands

alandsbeek.e: Named after a nearby stream and a farmhouse called De Beek (derived from beek (“brook, stream”)).

s.def: a hamlet in IJsselstein, Utrecht, Netherlands

achtersloot.e: First attested as aftersloten in 1267. Compound of Middle Dutch after (“back, behind”) and sloet (“ditch”). Named after a nearby watercourse running behind a set of plots of land.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Vijfheerenlanden, Utrecht, Netherlands

amaliastein.e: First attested as Ameliastein, gemeenlyk het huis in 't Viaensche Bosch geheten in 1725. From Amalia (a personal name) + -stein (“stone building, castle”). Named after a former castle built by Hendrik van Brederode (1531-1568) for his wife, Amalia von Neuenahr.

s.def: a village in De Ronde Venen, Utrecht, Netherlands

amstelhoek.e: Attested as Amstelhoek in 1936. Compound of the hydronym Amstel and hoek (“bend, corner”). Named after a nearby fortress.

s.def: a hamlet in De Ronde Venen, Utrecht, Netherlands

aan de zuwe.e: Attested as Aan-de-Zuwe in 1936. Derived from aan (“on”) and zuwe (“sideways levee”).

s.def: a former municipality of Utrecht, Netherlands

abcoude-baambrugge.e: Derived from the toponyms Abcoude and Baambrugge.

s.def: a former municipality of Utrecht, Netherlands

abcoude-proosdij.e: Derived from the toponym Abcoude and proosdij (“provosty”). The area occupied by the former municipality was originally a legal district belonging to the Provosty of Saint Peter of Utrecht.

s.def: a neighbourhood and former municipality of Utrecht, Utrecht, Netherlands

abstede.e: First attested as abbenstade around 1180. Compound of Old Dutch Abbe (a personal name) and statho (“bank, landing place”). Compare Abbestede.

s.def: a hamlet in Utrecht, Utrecht, Netherlands

alendorp.e: First attested as alendhorp in 1238. Compound of Middle Dutch Ale (a personal name) and dorp (“farmstead, village, subsidiary settlement”).

aldeneik.e: First attested as eike in 830. Compound of Old Dutch alt (“old”) and eike (“oak, Quercus robur”). The element oud was added at a later date to distinguish the settlement from Maaseik.

s.def: proclamation, announcement. Usually of the coming into force of a regulation or law

achel-statie.e: Compound of the toponym Achel and statie (“station”). The settlement formerly was the location of a train station.

amelterbos.e: Compound of the toponym Amelte and bos (“forest”).

asserbos.e: Compound of the toponym Assen and bos (“forest”).

aarsgewei.e: Compound of aars (“arse”) + gewei (“pair of antlers”). Cf. German Arschgeweih and English arse antlers.

The brand name Unox here is perhaps a reference to the term soepeend (literally “soup duck”), a semi-jocular term f...

alleenspraak.e: Calque of Latin soliloquium or Byzantine Greek μονόλογος (monólogos). Equivalent to alleen (“alone”) + spraak (“speech”).

alexanderparkiet.e: Named after Alexander the Great; the second element is parkiet.

amsterdamalbatros.e: From Amsterdam (“French island in the Indian Ocean”) + albatros (“albatross”).

atitlanfuut.e: From Nahuatl Atitlan (“lake in Guatemala”) + fuut (“grebe”).

anticiperen.e: From Middle Dutch anticiperen, from Latin anticipō (possibly via Middle French anticiper).

anioema.e: From a Tupian word, possibly via Portuguese anhuma, whence also anhima (see there for more).

autosomaal.e: Probably borrowed. By surface analysis, autosoom + -aal.

examples: De baby ziet er gezond uit, met mooie rode appelwangetjes.

appelwang.e: From appel (“apple”) + wang (“cheek”), referring to the red color of ripe apples.

ankeilen.e: Pseudo-Germanism, possibly derived from obsolete German ankeilen (“to secure something with a wedge”) or now chiefly colloquial Austrian German ankeilen (“to accost someone in order to sell them something”). It is unclear how the term acquired its present meaning in Dutch.

affectie.e: From Middle Dutch affectie, borrowed from Old French affection, from Latin affectiō, affectiōnem.

avondkoekoeksbloem.e: Compound of avond + koekoeksbloem. The flowers only open up fully when it starts getting dark (as with the nachtkoekoeksbloem, and in contrast with the dagkoekoeksbloem).

akelei.e: From Middle Dutch acoleie, acolie, ackeleie, akeleye, aculeie, from Medieval Latin aquilegia. Compare German Akelei.

s.def: an association of volunteers who ritually wash a corpse and prepare it for burial, in keeping with Afro-Surinamese traditions
examples: Als we met Bethanië - dat is de eerste Surinaamse afleggersvereniging - een overledene gaan verzorgen, komen we altijd met dertien, Jezus en de discipelen, ja, dat waren er dus ook dertien, we dragen witte uniformen, we zijn ongeveer drie uren met de overledene bezig en daar wordt steeds bij gezongen. Bethanië gaat ook mee naar het kerkhof om te zingen en de familie bij te staan.

Op de Drageman Dey worden de korpsen uit Para, Coronie en de negen in Paramaribo, verwacht. Ook personen van uitvaartverzorgingsbedrijven, afleggersverenigingen en kerkbesturen van de verschillende begraafplaatsen zijn uitgenodigd.

afleggersvereniging.e: Compound of aflegger (“person who prepares a corpse for burial”) + -s- + vereniging (“association, society”).

alaam.e: From Middle Dutch andlame. The first element (a-) is a reduced form of and- / ant-, the same prefix found in antwoord, the second (-laam) is cognate with English loom.

aberratie.e: Borrowed from French aberration, from Latin aberrātiō

animation (technique of making objects appear to move in motion pictures or computer graphics)

animatie.e: From animeren (“to animate”) + -atie. In the sense of the film-technique, the word is borrowed from English animation.

absentie.e: From Middle Dutch absentie, borrowed from Latin absentia.

animeren.e: Borrowed from French animer, itself borrowed from Latin animāre.
